0: <header>Managing Conflicting Economic and Environmental Metrics in Livestock Manure Management </header>
Yicheng Hu, Horacio Aguirre-Villegas, Rebecca A. Larson, and Victor M. Zavala*

ABSTRACT: Dairy farming is a multibillion USD industry that provides essential food products. At the same time, the millions of animals that this industry oversees generate a massive environ- mental footprint (affecting air, land, and water quality). Life Cycle Analysis Supply Chain Modeling Specifically, livestock manure is a carbon- and nutrient-rich waste Computational stream that is routinely used as fertilizer. This practice enables Framework nutrient recycling but also leads to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and to nutrient pollution of soils and waterbodies. Balanced Decision Mitigating these environmental impacts requires investment in Economic Analysis Utopia-tracking Methodology manure processing technologies and identifying and prioritizing investment strategies requires understanding inherent conflicts (trade-offs) and synergies that exist between economic and environmental impacts. In this work, we present a conflict analysis and resolution framework that integrates techno-economic analysis (TEA), life cycle assessment (LCA), and supply chain (SC) optimization. We use this framework to investigate deployment scenarios of manure processing pathways in the Upper Yahara watershed region of Wisconsin. We evaluate LCA (GHG emissions, ammonia emissions, fossil energy use, and nutrient pollution) and TEA metrics (cost and revenue) for different pathways that include manure collection, storage, application, and processing steps. The LCA and TEA metrics are embedded within a SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection and geographical placement and on product transport in the study area. A conflict resolution procedure is used to explore trade-offs associated with these decisions and to identify optimal compromise solutions that best balance trade-offs. Our results reveal that there are nonobvious conflicts and synergies between the explored metrics that can be exploited to mitigate multiple impacts simultaneously. We also find that the deployment of a diverse set of technologies is needed to fully resolve conflicts. The impact of emerging technologies (ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis) and government incentives is also discussed. KEYWORDS: conflict resolution, life cycle assessment, techno-economic analysis, supply chain optimization, livestock manure management

2: The dairy industry provides essential food products (e.g., milk and cheese) but also produces vast amounts of manure. To give some perspective, an average lactating dairy cow can produce 9500 L of milk and 9 000 L of manure in a year. Manure is a waste stream that is difficult to dispose of because it is highly diluted and distributed. Manure is typically disposed of via land application. This practice provides valuable nutrients for crop production (manure is a renewable fertilizer); however, land application of manure also promotes myriad environmental impacts.

Manure is the second largest source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. 2 Ammonia (NH3) emissions from manure can reach 70% of the excreted nitrogen (N) and can deposit into water ecosystems or transform into nitrous oxide (N2O), further contributing to climate change. 3 Manure management in livestock operations contributes 71% of the total NH3 emissions in the U.S.; this surpasses (by far) the 14% contribution of mineral fertilizers, with dairy alone represent-

ing 23% of these livestock emissions. 4 There is also evidence that NH3 emissions have negative impacts on biodiversity and can promote formation of particulate matter in the atmosphere. In addition to affecting air and water quality, N losses reduce availability of nutrients for crop production (increasing fertilizer costs for farmers). The deployment of manure processing technologies such as anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS) can help mitigate environmental impacts. AD can be used to recover biogas and this can be used to produce electricity. Biogas can also be purified and be used as renewable compressed natural gas (rCNG) for vehicle transportation.

The deployment of AD systems can reduce manure GHG emissions by more than 50%; this reduction is mostly due to mitigation of methane (CH4) emissions that originate from manure storage. Renewable power obtained from biogas can also offset carbon emissions from grid electricity.

Despite the many environmental benefits of manure processing technologies, their adoption is still limited by high capital, operational, and maintenance costs (e.g., investment might not be feasible for small or even some large operations). Strategies that reduce manure handling and technology costs and increase potential revenues (via sales of recovered products) are needed to promote the widespread adoption. Moreover, policy that incentivizes mitigation of environmental impacts (e.g., via nutrient and carbon credits) and/or recovery of value-added products is needed.

The environmental impacts of manure management path- ways can be quantified using life cycle assessment (LCA). The impacts of manure management have typically been explored as a subset of broader LCA studies that focus on the entire value chain of dairy products. Other LCA studies have evaluated biogas production from manure and a mix of agricultural feedstocks in order to assess GHG emissions and fossil fuel consumption. De Vries et al. evaluated the environmental impacts of manure separation by reverse osmosis; this study found higher overall impacts when compared to management of raw manure but also found reduced GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels with the addition of an AD system. Poeschl et 13,14 compared different AD systems that process single and mixed feedstocks. The study concluded that straw and corn silage produced the most biogas when evaluating single-feedstock technologies and combining municipal solid waste with agricultural and food residues produced the most biogas within codigestion technologies. Ebner et al. 15 conducted an LCA for an AD system that codigests dairy manure and industrial food waste; the study concluded that GHG emissions can be reduced by 70% (compared to conventional treatment of manure and food waste).

Most of these studies have not included an evaluation of inherent conflicts (trade-offs) that exist between environ- mental and economic metrics of different technology pathways. Process and systems engineering methodologies have been widely used in the field of environmental management to conduct systematic modeling and support decision-making over trade-offs. Applications reported include economic and environmental trade-offs in energy systems, process optimization of waste processing technologies, conversion of biomass to chemicals,2 design and improvement in food- energy-water nexus and circular economy, etc. Specifically for the organic waste management problem, supply chain (SC) network optimization has been used to describe and capture material transformation, logistical issues, and related economic and environmental aspects. Environmental and economic metrics can be further incorporated within conflict resolution (multiobjective) optimization models to systematically analyze inherent trade-offs and to identify solutions that best balance such trade-offs. A fundamental issue that arises here, however, is that the number of metrics to be explored is typically large. Typical LCA optimization studies have targeted up to three metrics; this is because exploring and visualizing the Pareto frontier is not straightforward in higher dimensions.24 29-31 Moreover, different stakeholders prioritize metrics differently
(e.g., water quality over air quality); as such, it is necessary to identify solutions that can best resolve conflicts.

In this work, we present a conflict analysis and resolution framework that integrates techno-economic analysis (TEA), LCA, and SC optimization. We use this framework to investigate deployment scenarios for technology pathways in the Upper Yahara watershed region of Wisconsin. Here, we evaluate multiple important LCA metrics (GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, fossil energy use, and nitrogen, phosphorus, and photassium, N/P/K, release) and TEA metrics (cost and revenue) for different pathways that include manure collection, storage, application, and processing (AD and SLS). The metrics are embedded within a comprehensive SC model that makes decisions on technology selection, sizing, placement, and product transport in the study area. A conflict resolution procedure is used to explore trade-offs associated with these decisions and to identify optimal compromise solutions that best balance trade-offs. Our results shed light into key manure processing technologies and management practices that are needed to mitigate specific impacts. The results also reveal that there exist nonobvious conflicts and synergies between the explored metrics that can be exploited to mitigate multiple impacts simultaneously. We also find that the deployment of a diverse set of pathways is needed to fully resolve conflicts. The impact of emerging technologies (ultrafiltration, UF, and reverse osmosis, RO) and government incentives on the resolution of conflicts is also discussed.

Manure is the second largest source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. 2 Ammonia (NH3) emissions from manure can reach 70% of the excreted nitrogen (N) and can deposit into water ecosystems or transform into nitrous oxide (N2O), further contributing to climate change. 3 Manure management in livestock operations contributes 71% of the total NH3 emissions in the U.S.; this surpasses (by far) the 14% contribution of mineral fertilizers, with dairy alone represent-

ing 23% of these livestock emissions. 4 There is also evidence that NH3 emissions have negative impacts on biodiversity and can promote formation of particulate matter in the atmosphere. In addition to affecting air and water quality, N losses reduce availability of nutrients for crop production (increasing fertilizer costs for farmers). The deployment of manure processing technologies such as anaerobic digestion (AD) and solid-liquid separation (SLS) can help mitigate environmental impacts. AD can be used to recover biogas and this can be used to produce electricity. Biogas can also be purified and be used as renewable compressed natural gas (rCNG) for vehicle transportation.

The deployment of AD systems can reduce manure GHG emissions by more than 50%; this reduction is mostly due to mitigation of methane (CH4) emissions that originate from manure storage. Renewable power obtained from biogas can also offset carbon emissions from grid electricity.

Despite the many environmental benefits of manure processing technologies, their adoption is still limited by high capital, operational, and maintenance costs (e.g., investment might not be feasible for small or even some large operations). Strategies that reduce manure handling and technology costs and increase potential revenues (via sales of recovered products) are needed to promote the widespread adoption. Moreover, policy that incentivizes mitigation of environmental impacts (e.g., via nutrient and carbon credits) and/or recovery of value-added products is needed.

The environmental impacts of manure management path- ways can be quantified using life cycle assessment (LCA). The impacts of manure management have typically been explored as a subset of broader LCA studies that focus on the entire value chain of dairy products. Other LCA studies have evaluated biogas production from manure and a mix of agricultural feedstocks in order to assess GHG emissions and fossil fuel consumption. De Vries et al. evaluated the environmental impacts of manure separation by reverse osmosis; this study found higher overall impacts when compared to management of raw manure but also found reduced GHG emissions and depletion of fossil fuels with the addition of an AD system. Poeschl et 13,14 compared different AD systems that process single and mixed feedstocks. The study concluded that straw and corn silage produced the most biogas when evaluating single-feedstock technologies and combining municipal solid waste with agricultural and food residues produced the most biogas within codigestion technologies. Ebner et al. 15 conducted an LCA for an AD system that codigests dairy manure and industrial food waste; the study concluded that GHG emissions can be reduced by 70% (compared to conventional treatment of manure and food waste).

Most of these studies have not included an evaluation of inherent conflicts (trade-offs) that exist between environ- mental and economic metrics of different technology pathways. Process and systems engineering methodologies have been widely used in the field of environmental management to conduct systematic modeling and support decision-making over trade-offs. Applications reported include economic and environmental trade-offs in energy systems, process optimization of waste processing technologies, conversion of biomass to chemicals,2 design and improvement in food- energy-water nexus and circular economy, etc. Specifically for the organic waste management problem, supply chain (SC) network optimization has been used to describe and capture material transformation, logistical issues, and related economic and environmental aspects. Environmental and economic metrics can be further incorporated within conflict resolution (multiobjective) optimization models to systematically analyze inherent trade-offs and to identify solutions that best balance such trade-offs. A fundamental issue that arises here, however, is that the number of metrics to be explored is typically large. Typical LCA optimization studies have targeted up to three metrics; this is because exploring and visualizing the Pareto frontier is not straightforward in higher dimensions.24 29-31 Moreover, different stakeholders prioritize metrics differently
(e.g., water quality over air quality); as such, it is necessary to identify solutions that can best resolve conflicts.

In this work, we present a conflict analysis and resolution framework that integrates techno-economic analysis (TEA), LCA, and SC optimization. We use this framework to investigate deployment scenarios for technology pathways in the Upper Yahara watershed region of Wisconsin. Here, we evaluate multiple important LCA metrics (GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, fossil energy use, and nitrogen, phosphorus, and photassium, N/P/K, release) and TEA metrics (cost and revenue) for different pathways that include manure collection, storage, application, and processing (AD and SLS). The metrics are embedded within a comprehensive SC model that makes decisions on technology selection, sizing, placement, and product transport in the study area. A conflict resolution procedure is used to explore trade-offs associated with these decisions and to identify optimal compromise solutions that best balance trade-offs. Our results shed light into key manure processing technologies and management practices that are needed to mitigate specific impacts. The results also reveal that there exist nonobvious conflicts and synergies between the explored metrics that can be exploited to mitigate multiple impacts simultaneously. We also find that the deployment of a diverse set of pathways is needed to fully resolve conflicts. The impact of emerging technologies (ultrafiltration, UF, and reverse osmosis, RO) and government incentives on the resolution of conflicts is also discussed.

9: The computational framework proposed includes the following components: quantification of environmental and economic metrics, supply chain model, and conflict resolution procedure. The environmental and economic metrics for diverse manure management pathways are evaluated using LCA and TEA approaches. These metrics are then used in an SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection/sizing/ placement, transportation, and production. Trade-offs are explored by solving the SC model repetitively in order to minimize/maximize individual metrics and construct a pay-off matrix. A conflict resolution procedure is then used to identify an optimal compromise solution that best balances the individual metrics. We note that the main methodological novelty of this study is to combine multiple computational components to quantify and resolve the conflicts between many economic and environmental metrics.

Life Cycle Assessment. An LCA model³ was used to estimate (i) GHG emissions, (ii) NH3 emissions, and (iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) for dairy and beef manure management pathways. These environmental metrics include manure collection, storage, processing, and land application steps. In addition, nutrients (N, P, and K) reaching the land were tracked. We conducted analysis for a dairy farm with 2306 animal units (1 AU = 1000 pound of animal) that manages 1000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers, and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and for a beed farm with 2478 AU that manages 1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle. Manure character- istics were estimated for each animal type for dairy33 and beef34 and aggregated for the herd (Table S1).

A total of 11 technology pathways were modeled for both dairy and beef farms (Table 1). Manure is collected from the barn in all pathways and then is either directly land applied, stored prior to land application, or processed -via sand
Table 1. Summary of the Operational Steps Included in Each Technology Pathway

technology|processing unit|storage type|land application method 1|none|none|surface 2|none|natural crust|surface 3|sand recovery|uncovered|surface 4|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|surface 5|sand recovery + AD + SLS|uncovered|surface 6|sand recovery + SLS|uncovered|surface 7|none|covered|injection 8|sand recovery + AD|covered|surface 9|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|injection 10|sand recovery + AD|covered|injection 11|sand recovery + AD + SLS + UF + RO|uncovered|surface

recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + UF + RO)- and then land applied. All technical and economic data for the clean water system throughout all stages (SLS, UF and RO) was obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S. Manure storage is considered with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 1 presents a summary of each pathway; in this context, a technology is defined as a pathway containing different manure handling operation units.

GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include CH4, direct and indirect N2O from manure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on- farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, for a 100 year horizon. NH3 (expressed in 35 kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage, and land application, and DFF (expressed in MJ) originates from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

Manure handling operations include collection, storage, and land application steps. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate (conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium36) of 16% is assumed for the given storage duration. An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher total solids in manure, which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but 37 increases N2O emissions. Liner covers of the storage system (a lined pond) are also modeled. Manure is agitated and then land-applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions. In other words, land application requires hauling and thus consumes fossil fuels and generates carbon emissions.

Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separation efficiencies reported in Aguirre- Villegas et al. A plug-flow digester is used to produce biogas 38 considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from biogas (65% CH4 with a lower heating value of 36 MJ/ m³) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. We note that the final CH4 production is assumed on the basis of typical volumetric compositions, which includes all losses as it is based on empirical data. 39 The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal

energy is lost. In addition, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity. Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization during the digestion process. It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

Techno-Economic Analysis. Techno-economic analysis was conducted to evaluate the investment and operational costs of each technology pathway in Table 1. The first step in TEA is to construct a general mass balance (product transformation relationships) based on the LCA model and literature data, and it is provided in Tables S3 and S4. The investment cost of each technology pathway mainly involves the cost of corresponding equipment, and the operational cost mainly involves utility and maintenance costs. Both of those costs are estimated by scaling from data reported on the basis of the mass balance. We note that the purpose of this section is to obtain estimated costs within reasonable orders of magnitude instead of accurate cost analysis. We provide a brief overview below, and readers can refer to the Supporting Information for more details on the TEA methodology.

Manure needs to be collected first before being processed, and a standard manure collection cost, 0.3 USD/cow/day is applied to all pathways. Collected manure can be stored and land applied (pathways 2 and 7). The investment cost of storage systems includes a fixed construction cost and a proportional cost (related to storage volume). For a storage system with cover, a fixed average additional covering cost is applied. The stored manure retains more than 99% of its original weight, and the loss is mainly due to the emission of gas and evaporation.

Sand recovery systems are needed before AD to adjust the solid content by diluting the manure and increase the stream weight. Due to the limited cost data of the sand recovery system, we assume that both investment and operational costs are proportional to the processing amount (facility size). 42 The sand-recovered manure can be used for AD, which converts

manure into digestate and biogas, and in this analysis, generated biogas is converted to electricity by a generator. The investment cost of the digester is obtained using the so- called 6/10 power-law regression to capture the economies of scale.4 The investment cost of the electricity generator is set as 2/3 of the corresponding digester (typical estimate used in the literature43,44 The operational cost of the AD system includes maintenance cost (which represents 10% of invest- ment) and biogas cleaning cost, which is proportional to the amount of biogas generated. The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

Supply Chain Model. We developed an SC model that aims to systematically make decisions on which technologies to deploy and at which location. The model also makes a decision on transport of products (e.g., raw manure) and derived products (e.g., recovered nutrients) between geographical locations. In other words, the SC can be seen as a cooperative system that can exchange products across farms in order to minimize costs and mitigate environmental impacts.

The SC optimization model used is extended from that in ref 46 by integrating multiple economic and environmental metrics; this allows us to evaluate the performance of the SC from multiple perspectives.

In the SC model, we have a set of geographical nodes (locations), a set of materials, and a set of technologies (technology pathways). Materials can be transported between geographical nodes and transformed in technologies, and technologies can be installed in certain geographical nodes. The main constraints in the optimization model include material balances and conversion constraints at each location, operational capacity constraints, and logical constraints for technology selection and placement.

The SC model also calculates the total (system-wide) TEA and LCA metrics for the entire SC. These metrics contain impacts associated with technology pathways and trans- portation operations. The decision variables of the SC model include product transport flows and technology selection and placement. The decision variables are a combination of continuous (e.g., flows) and discrete (e.g., selection) variables; the SC model is thus a mixed-integer optimization problem. By adjusting the decision variables, the SC model seeks to minimize the TEA and LCA metrics simultaneously. In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Conflict Analysis and Resolution. To facilitate the discussion, we denote the collection of decision variables of the SC model as X, and the system-wide TEA and LCA metrics as f1(x),f2(x), ,fk(x), where k = III + 1. We further denote the feasible set of the SC model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

The economic and environmental metrics considered can be conflicting (decreasing one increases the other). Such conflicts (trade-offs) can be addressed using multiobjective optimiza- tion techniques. These techniques traditionally aim to compute the so-called Pareto frontier (PF). The PF contains all solutions that cannot be improved in the sense that one cannot further improve an objective without sacrificing another

objective. The PF enables visualization of trade-offs between different metrics (but this is difficult when more than three metrics are explored). A final decision is often selected from the PF based on expert knowledge or preferences by a decision-maker. This choice can be ambiguous because it relies on the priorities of the decision-maker (what metrics are prioritized). Moreover, to obtain the entire PF, it is necessary to solve an extremely large number of optimization problems (e.g., by putting different weights or budgets on the different metrics). For instance, in the multistage e-constraint method,4 47 one solves the following set of problems:

Here, Ej is some predefined threshold for objective fi(x). This method is not computationally feasible in our context because computing a single point on the PF requires solving the entire SC problem, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of metrics increases. Sampling strategies are also often necessary to capture points on different regions of the PF, which can require additional computational time. Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:

The overall conflict resolution procedure is illustrated in Figure 1. This approach consists of the utopia point computation, pay-off matrix computation, and OCS computa- tion. The procedure is illustrated graphically in Figure 2 for a

setting with a couple of metrics. The overall procedure needs to solve the entire SC model a total of k2 + 1 times; each of these instances is a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem. In the case study considered in the following section, the entire procedure can take weeks to complete. This highlights the complexity involved in systematically assessing trade-offs in manure management.

Techno-Economic Analysis. Techno-economic analysis was conducted to evaluate the investment and operational costs of each technology pathway in Table 1. The first step in TEA is to construct a general mass balance (product transformation relationships) based on the LCA model and literature data, and it is provided in Tables S3 and S4. The investment cost of each technology pathway mainly involves the cost of corresponding equipment, and the operational cost mainly involves utility and maintenance costs. Both of those costs are estimated by scaling from data reported on the basis of the mass balance. We note that the purpose of this section is to obtain estimated costs within reasonable orders of magnitude instead of accurate cost analysis. We provide a brief overview below, and readers can refer to the Supporting Information for more details on the TEA methodology.

Manure needs to be collected first before being processed, and a standard manure collection cost, 0.3 USD/cow/day is applied to all pathways. Collected manure can be stored and land applied (pathways 2 and 7). The investment cost of storage systems includes a fixed construction cost and a proportional cost (related to storage volume). For a storage system with cover, a fixed average additional covering cost is applied. The stored manure retains more than 99% of its original weight, and the loss is mainly due to the emission of gas and evaporation.

Sand recovery systems are needed before AD to adjust the solid content by diluting the manure and increase the stream weight. Due to the limited cost data of the sand recovery system, we assume that both investment and operational costs are proportional to the processing amount (facility size). 42 The sand-recovered manure can be used for AD, which converts

manure into digestate and biogas, and in this analysis, generated biogas is converted to electricity by a generator. The investment cost of the digester is obtained using the so- called 6/10 power-law regression to capture the economies of scale.4 The investment cost of the electricity generator is set as 2/3 of the corresponding digester (typical estimate used in the literature43,44 The operational cost of the AD system includes maintenance cost (which represents 10% of invest- ment) and biogas cleaning cost, which is proportional to the amount of biogas generated. The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

Supply Chain Model. We developed an SC model that aims to systematically make decisions on which technologies to deploy and at which location. The model also makes a decision on transport of products (e.g., raw manure) and derived products (e.g., recovered nutrients) between geographical locations. In other words, the SC can be seen as a cooperative system that can exchange products across farms in order to minimize costs and mitigate environmental impacts.

The SC optimization model used is extended from that in ref 46 by integrating multiple economic and environmental metrics; this allows us to evaluate the performance of the SC from multiple perspectives.

In the SC model, we have a set of geographical nodes (locations), a set of materials, and a set of technologies (technology pathways). Materials can be transported between geographical nodes and transformed in technologies, and technologies can be installed in certain geographical nodes. The main constraints in the optimization model include material balances and conversion constraints at each location, operational capacity constraints, and logical constraints for technology selection and placement.

The SC model also calculates the total (system-wide) TEA and LCA metrics for the entire SC. These metrics contain impacts associated with technology pathways and trans- portation operations. The decision variables of the SC model include product transport flows and technology selection and placement. The decision variables are a combination of continuous (e.g., flows) and discrete (e.g., selection) variables; the SC model is thus a mixed-integer optimization problem. By adjusting the decision variables, the SC model seeks to minimize the TEA and LCA metrics simultaneously. In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Conflict Analysis and Resolution. To facilitate the discussion, we denote the collection of decision variables of the SC model as X, and the system-wide TEA and LCA metrics as f1(x),f2(x), ,fk(x), where k = III + 1. We further denote the feasible set of the SC model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

The economic and environmental metrics considered can be conflicting (decreasing one increases the other). Such conflicts (trade-offs) can be addressed using multiobjective optimiza- tion techniques. These techniques traditionally aim to compute the so-called Pareto frontier (PF). The PF contains all solutions that cannot be improved in the sense that one cannot further improve an objective without sacrificing another

objective. The PF enables visualization of trade-offs between different metrics (but this is difficult when more than three metrics are explored). A final decision is often selected from the PF based on expert knowledge or preferences by a decision-maker. This choice can be ambiguous because it relies on the priorities of the decision-maker (what metrics are prioritized). Moreover, to obtain the entire PF, it is necessary to solve an extremely large number of optimization problems (e.g., by putting different weights or budgets on the different metrics). For instance, in the multistage e-constraint method,4 47 one solves the following set of problems:

Here, Ej is some predefined threshold for objective fi(x). This method is not computationally feasible in our context because computing a single point on the PF requires solving the entire SC problem, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of metrics increases. Sampling strategies are also often necessary to capture points on different regions of the PF, which can require additional computational time. Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:

The overall conflict resolution procedure is illustrated in Figure 1. This approach consists of the utopia point computation, pay-off matrix computation, and OCS computa- tion. The procedure is illustrated graphically in Figure 2 for a

setting with a couple of metrics. The overall procedure needs to solve the entire SC model a total of k2 + 1 times; each of these instances is a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem. 11: The computational framework proposed includes the following components: quantification of environmental and economic metrics, supply chain model, and conflict resolution procedure. The environmental and economic metrics for diverse manure management pathways are evaluated using LCA and TEA approaches. These metrics are then used in an SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection/sizing/ placement, transportation, and production. Trade-offs are explored by solving the SC model repetitively in order to minimize/maximize individual metrics and construct a pay-off matrix. A conflict resolution procedure is then used to identify an optimal compromise solution that best balances the individual metrics. We note that the main methodological novelty of this study is to combine multiple computational components to quantify and resolve the conflicts between many economic and environmental metrics.

Life Cycle Assessment. An LCA model³ was used to estimate (i) GHG emissions, (ii) NH3 emissions, and (iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) for dairy and beef manure management pathways. These environmental metrics include manure collection, storage, processing, and land application steps. In addition, nutrients (N, P, and K) reaching the land were tracked. We conducted analysis for a dairy farm with 2306 animal units (1 AU = 1000 pound of animal) that manages 1000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers, and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and for a beed farm with 2478 AU that manages 1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle. Manure character- istics were estimated for each animal type for dairy33 and beef34 and aggregated for the herd (Table S1).

A total of 11 technology pathways were modeled for both dairy and beef farms (Table 1). Manure is collected from the barn in all pathways and then is either directly land applied, stored prior to land application, or processed -via sand
Table 1. Summary of the Operational Steps Included in Each Technology Pathway

technology|processing unit|storage type|land application method 1|none|none|surface 2|none|natural crust|surface 3|sand recovery|uncovered|surface 4|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|surface 5|sand recovery + AD + SLS|uncovered|surface 6|sand recovery + SLS|uncovered|surface 7|none|covered|injection 8|sand recovery + AD|covered|surface 9|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|injection 10|sand recovery + AD|covered|injection 11|sand recovery + AD + SLS + UF + RO|uncovered|surface

recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + UF + RO)- and then land applied. All technical and economic data for the clean water system throughout all stages (SLS, UF and RO) was obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S. Manure storage is considered with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 1 presents a summary of each pathway; in this context, a technology is defined as a pathway containing different manure handling operation units.

GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include CH4, direct and indirect N2O from manure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on- farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, for a 100 year horizon. NH3 (expressed in 35 kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage, and land application, and DFF (expressed in MJ) originates from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

Manure handling operations include collection, storage, and land application steps. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate (conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium36) of 16% is assumed for the given storage duration. An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher total solids in manure, which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but 37 increases N2O emissions. Liner covers of the storage system (a lined pond) are also modeled. Manure is agitated and then land-applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions. In other words, land application requires hauling and thus consumes fossil fuels and generates carbon emissions.

Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separation efficiencies reported in Aguirre- Villegas et al. A plug-flow digester is used to produce biogas 38 considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from biogas (65% CH4 with a lower heating value of 36 MJ/ m³) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. We note that the final CH4 production is assumed on the basis of typical volumetric compositions, which includes all losses as it is based on empirical data. 39 The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal

energy is lost. In addition, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity. Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization during the digestion process. It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

Supply Chain Model. We developed an SC model that aims to systematically make decisions on which technologies to deploy and at which location. The model also makes a decision on transport of products (e.g., raw manure) and derived products (e.g., recovered nutrients) between geographical locations. In other words, the SC can be seen as a cooperative system that can exchange products across farms in order to minimize costs and mitigate environmental impacts.

The SC optimization model used is extended from that in ref 46 by integrating multiple economic and environmental metrics; this allows us to evaluate the performance of the SC from multiple perspectives.

In the SC model, we have a set of geographical nodes (locations), a set of materials, and a set of technologies (technology pathways). Materials can be transported between geographical nodes and transformed in technologies, and technologies can be installed in certain geographical nodes. The main constraints in the optimization model include material balances and conversion constraints at each location, operational capacity constraints, and logical constraints for technology selection and placement.

The SC model also calculates the total (system-wide) TEA and LCA metrics for the entire SC. These metrics contain impacts associated with technology pathways and trans- portation operations. The decision variables of the SC model include product transport flows and technology selection and placement. The decision variables are a combination of continuous (e.g., flows) and discrete (e.g., selection) variables; the SC model is thus a mixed-integer optimization problem. By adjusting the decision variables, the SC model seeks to minimize the TEA and LCA metrics simultaneously. In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Conflict Analysis and Resolution. To facilitate the discussion, we denote the collection of decision variables of the SC model as X, and the system-wide TEA and LCA metrics as f1(x),f2(x), ,fk(x), where k = III + 1. We further denote the feasible set of the SC model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

The economic and environmental metrics considered can be conflicting (decreasing one increases the other). Such conflicts (trade-offs) can be addressed using multiobjective optimiza- tion techniques. These techniques traditionally aim to compute the so-called Pareto frontier (PF). The PF contains all solutions that cannot be improved in the sense that one cannot further improve an objective without sacrificing another

objective. The PF enables visualization of trade-offs between different metrics (but this is difficult when more than three metrics are explored). A final decision is often selected from the PF based on expert knowledge or preferences by a decision-maker. This choice can be ambiguous because it relies on the priorities of the decision-maker (what metrics are prioritized). Moreover, to obtain the entire PF, it is necessary to solve an extremely large number of optimization problems (e.g., by putting different weights or budgets on the different metrics). For instance, in the multistage e-constraint method,4 47 one solves the following set of problems:

Here, Ej is some predefined threshold for objective fi(x). This method is not computationally feasible in our context because computing a single point on the PF requires solving the entire SC problem, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of metrics increases. Sampling strategies are also often necessary to capture points on different regions of the PF, which can require additional computational time. Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:

This procedure for constructing the pay- off matrix P is known as lexicographic optimization. The pay- off matrix provides also valuable information on the inherent trade-offs between the objectives.

The overall conflict resolution procedure is illustrated in Figure 1. This approach consists of the utopia point computation, pay-off matrix computation, and OCS computa- tion. The procedure is illustrated graphically in Figure 2 for a

13: The computational framework proposed includes the following components: quantification of environmental and economic metrics, supply chain model, and conflict resolution procedure. The environmental and economic metrics for diverse manure management pathways are evaluated using LCA and TEA approaches. These metrics are then used in an SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection/sizing/ placement, transportation, and production. Trade-offs are explored by solving the SC model repetitively in order to minimize/maximize individual metrics and construct a pay-off matrix. A conflict resolution procedure is then used to identify an optimal compromise solution that best balances the individual metrics. We note that the main methodological novelty of this study is to combine multiple computational components to quantify and resolve the conflicts between many economic and environmental metrics.

Life Cycle Assessment. An LCA model³ was used to estimate (i) GHG emissions, (ii) NH3 emissions, and (iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) for dairy and beef manure management pathways. These environmental metrics include manure collection, storage, processing, and land application steps. In addition, nutrients (N, P, and K) reaching the land were tracked. We conducted analysis for a dairy farm with 2306 animal units (1 AU = 1000 pound of animal) that manages 1000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers, and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and for a beed farm with 2478 AU that manages 1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle. Manure character- istics were estimated for each animal type for dairy33 and beef34 and aggregated for the herd (Table S1).

A total of 11 technology pathways were modeled for both dairy and beef farms (Table 1). Manure is collected from the barn in all pathways and then is either directly land applied, stored prior to land application, or processed -via sand
Table 1. Summary of the Operational Steps Included in Each Technology Pathway

technology|processing unit|storage type|land application method 1|none|none|surface 2|none|natural crust|surface 3|sand recovery|uncovered|surface 4|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|surface 5|sand recovery + AD + SLS|uncovered|surface 6|sand recovery + SLS|uncovered|surface 7|none|covered|injection 8|sand recovery + AD|covered|surface 9|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|injection 10|sand recovery + AD|covered|injection 11|sand recovery + AD + SLS + UF + RO|uncovered|surface

recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + UF + RO)- and then land applied. All technical and economic data for the clean water system throughout all stages (SLS, UF and RO) was obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S. Manure storage is considered with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 1 presents a summary of each pathway; in this context, a technology is defined as a pathway containing different manure handling operation units.

GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include CH4, direct and indirect N2O from manure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on- farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, for a 100 year horizon. NH3 (expressed in 35 kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage, and land application, and DFF (expressed in MJ) originates from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

Manure handling operations include collection, storage, and land application steps. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate (conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium36) of 16% is assumed for the given storage duration. An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher total solids in manure, which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but 37 increases N2O emissions. Liner covers of the storage system (a lined pond) are also modeled. Manure is agitated and then land-applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions. In other words, land application requires hauling and thus consumes fossil fuels and generates carbon emissions.

Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separation efficiencies reported in Aguirre- Villegas et al. A plug-flow digester is used to produce biogas 38 considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from biogas (65% CH4 with a lower heating value of 36 MJ/ m³) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. We note that the final CH4 production is assumed on the basis of typical volumetric compositions, which includes all losses as it is based on empirical data. 39 The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal

energy is lost. In addition, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity. Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization during the digestion process. It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

Techno-Economic Analysis. Techno-economic analysis was conducted to evaluate the investment and operational costs of each technology pathway in Table 1. The first step in TEA is to construct a general mass balance (product transformation relationships) based on the LCA model and literature data, and it is provided in Tables S3 and S4. The investment cost of each technology pathway mainly involves the cost of corresponding equipment, and the operational cost mainly involves utility and maintenance costs. Both of those costs are estimated by scaling from data reported on the basis of the mass balance. We note that the purpose of this section is to obtain estimated costs within reasonable orders of magnitude instead of accurate cost analysis. We provide a brief overview below, and readers can refer to the Supporting Information for more details on the TEA methodology.

Manure needs to be collected first before being processed, and a standard manure collection cost, 0.3 USD/cow/day is applied to all pathways. Collected manure can be stored and land applied (pathways 2 and 7). The investment cost of storage systems includes a fixed construction cost and a proportional cost (related to storage volume). For a storage system with cover, a fixed average additional covering cost is applied. The stored manure retains more than 99% of its original weight, and the loss is mainly due to the emission of gas and evaporation.

Sand recovery systems are needed before AD to adjust the solid content by diluting the manure and increase the stream weight. Due to the limited cost data of the sand recovery system, we assume that both investment and operational costs are proportional to the processing amount (facility size). 42 The sand-recovered manure can be used for AD, which converts

manure into digestate and biogas, and in this analysis, generated biogas is converted to electricity by a generator. The investment cost of the digester is obtained using the so- called 6/10 power-law regression to capture the economies of scale.4 The investment cost of the electricity generator is set as 2/3 of the corresponding digester (typical estimate used in the literature43,44 The operational cost of the AD system includes maintenance cost (which represents 10% of invest- ment) and biogas cleaning cost, which is proportional to the amount of biogas generated. The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Conflict Analysis and Resolution. To facilitate the discussion, we denote the collection of decision variables of the SC model as X, and the system-wide TEA and LCA metrics as f1(x),f2(x), ,fk(x), where k = III + 1. We further denote the feasible set of the SC model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

The economic and environmental metrics considered can be conflicting (decreasing one increases the other). Such conflicts (trade-offs) can be addressed using multiobjective optimiza- tion techniques. These techniques traditionally aim to compute the so-called Pareto frontier (PF). The PF contains all solutions that cannot be improved in the sense that one cannot further improve an objective without sacrificing another

objective. The PF enables visualization of trade-offs between different metrics (but this is difficult when more than three metrics are explored). A final decision is often selected from the PF based on expert knowledge or preferences by a decision-maker. This choice can be ambiguous because it relies on the priorities of the decision-maker (what metrics are prioritized). Moreover, to obtain the entire PF, it is necessary to solve an extremely large number of optimization problems (e.g., by putting different weights or budgets on the different metrics). For instance, in the multistage e-constraint method,4 47 one solves the following set of problems:

Here, Ej is some predefined threshold for objective fi(x). This method is not computationally feasible in our context because computing a single point on the PF requires solving the entire SC problem, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of metrics increases. Sampling strategies are also often necessary to capture points on different regions of the PF, which can require additional computational time. Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:

15: The computational framework proposed includes the following components: quantification of environmental and economic metrics, supply chain model, and conflict resolution procedure. The environmental and economic metrics for diverse manure management pathways are evaluated using LCA and TEA approaches. These metrics are then used in an SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection/sizing/ placement, transportation, and production. Trade-offs are explored by solving the SC model repetitively in order to minimize/maximize individual metrics and construct a pay-off matrix. A conflict resolution procedure is then used to identify an optimal compromise solution that best balances the individual metrics. We note that the main methodological novelty of this study is to combine multiple computational components to quantify and resolve the conflicts between many economic and environmental metrics.

Life Cycle Assessment. An LCA model³ was used to estimate (i) GHG emissions, (ii) NH3 emissions, and (iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) for dairy and beef manure management pathways. These environmental metrics include manure collection, storage, processing, and land application steps. In addition, nutrients (N, P, and K) reaching the land were tracked. We conducted analysis for a dairy farm with 2306 animal units (1 AU = 1000 pound of animal) that manages 1000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers, and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and for a beed farm with 2478 AU that manages 1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle. Manure character- istics were estimated for each animal type for dairy33 and beef34 and aggregated for the herd (Table S1).

A total of 11 technology pathways were modeled for both dairy and beef farms (Table 1). Manure is collected from the barn in all pathways and then is either directly land applied, stored prior to land application, or processed -via sand
Table 1. Summary of the Operational Steps Included in Each Technology Pathway

technology|processing unit|storage type|land application method 1|none|none|surface 2|none|natural crust|surface 3|sand recovery|uncovered|surface 4|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|surface 5|sand recovery + AD + SLS|uncovered|surface 6|sand recovery + SLS|uncovered|surface 7|none|covered|injection 8|sand recovery + AD|covered|surface 9|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|injection 10|sand recovery + AD|covered|injection 11|sand recovery + AD + SLS + UF + RO|uncovered|surface

recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + UF + RO)- and then land applied. All technical and economic data for the clean water system throughout all stages (SLS, UF and RO) was obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S. Manure storage is considered with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 1 presents a summary of each pathway; in this context, a technology is defined as a pathway containing different manure handling operation units.

GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include CH4, direct and indirect N2O from manure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on- farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, for a 100 year horizon. NH3 (expressed in 35 kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage, and land application, and DFF (expressed in MJ) originates from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

Manure handling operations include collection, storage, and land application steps. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate (conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium36) of 16% is assumed for the given storage duration. An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher total solids in manure, which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but 37 increases N2O emissions. Liner covers of the storage system (a lined pond) are also modeled. Manure is agitated and then land-applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions. In other words, land application requires hauling and thus consumes fossil fuels and generates carbon emissions.

Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separation efficiencies reported in Aguirre- Villegas et al. A plug-flow digester is used to produce biogas 38 considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from biogas (65% CH4 with a lower heating value of 36 MJ/ m³) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. We note that the final CH4 production is assumed on the basis of typical volumetric compositions, which includes all losses as it is based on empirical data. 39 The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal

energy is lost. In addition, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity. Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization during the digestion process. It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

Techno-Economic Analysis. Techno-economic analysis was conducted to evaluate the investment and operational costs of each technology pathway in Table 1. The first step in TEA is to construct a general mass balance (product transformation relationships) based on the LCA model and literature data, and it is provided in Tables S3 and S4. The investment cost of each technology pathway mainly involves the cost of corresponding equipment, and the operational cost mainly involves utility and maintenance costs. Both of those costs are estimated by scaling from data reported on the basis of the mass balance. We note that the purpose of this section is to obtain estimated costs within reasonable orders of magnitude instead of accurate cost analysis. We provide a brief overview below, and readers can refer to the Supporting Information for more details on the TEA methodology.

Manure needs to be collected first before being processed, and a standard manure collection cost, 0.3 USD/cow/day is applied to all pathways. Collected manure can be stored and land applied (pathways 2 and 7). The investment cost of storage systems includes a fixed construction cost and a proportional cost (related to storage volume). For a storage system with cover, a fixed average additional covering cost is applied. The stored manure retains more than 99% of its original weight, and the loss is mainly due to the emission of gas and evaporation.

Sand recovery systems are needed before AD to adjust the solid content by diluting the manure and increase the stream weight. Due to the limited cost data of the sand recovery system, we assume that both investment and operational costs are proportional to the processing amount (facility size). 42 The sand-recovered manure can be used for AD, which converts

manure into digestate and biogas, and in this analysis, generated biogas is converted to electricity by a generator. The investment cost of the digester is obtained using the so- called 6/10 power-law regression to capture the economies of scale.4 The investment cost of the electricity generator is set as 2/3 of the corresponding digester (typical estimate used in the literature43,44 The operational cost of the AD system includes maintenance cost (which represents 10% of invest- ment) and biogas cleaning cost, which is proportional to the amount of biogas generated. The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

Supply Chain Model. We developed an SC model that aims to systematically make decisions on which technologies to deploy and at which location. The model also makes a decision on transport of products (e.g., raw manure) and derived products (e.g., recovered nutrients) between geographical locations. In other words, the SC can be seen as a cooperative system that can exchange products across farms in order to minimize costs and mitigate environmental impacts.

The SC optimization model used is extended from that in ref 46 by integrating multiple economic and environmental metrics; this allows us to evaluate the performance of the SC from multiple perspectives.

In the SC model, we have a set of geographical nodes (locations), a set of materials, and a set of technologies (technology pathways). Materials can be transported between geographical nodes and transformed in technologies, and technologies can be installed in certain geographical nodes. The main constraints in the optimization model include material balances and conversion constraints at each location, operational capacity constraints, and logical constraints for technology selection and placement.

The SC model also calculates the total (system-wide) TEA and LCA metrics for the entire SC. These metrics contain impacts associated with technology pathways and trans- portation operations. The decision variables of the SC model include product transport flows and technology selection and placement. The decision variables are a combination of continuous (e.g., flows) and discrete (e.g., selection) variables; the SC model is thus a mixed-integer optimization problem. By adjusting the decision variables, the SC model seeks to minimize the TEA and LCA metrics simultaneously. In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:
