It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

Techno-Economic Analysis. Techno-economic analysis was conducted to evaluate the investment and operational costs of each technology pathway in Table 1. The first step in TEA is to construct a general mass balance (product transformation relationships) based on the LCA model and literature data, and it is provided in Tables S3 and S4. The investment cost of each technology pathway mainly involves the cost of corresponding equipment, and the operational cost mainly involves utility and maintenance costs. Both of those costs are estimated by scaling from data reported on the basis of the mass balance. We note that the purpose of this section is to obtain estimated costs within reasonable orders of magnitude instead of accurate cost analysis. We provide a brief overview below, and readers can refer to the Supporting Information for more details on the TEA methodology.

Manure needs to be collected first before being processed, and a standard manure collection cost, 0.3 USD/cow/day is applied to all pathways. Collected manure can be stored and land applied (pathways 2 and 7). The investment cost of storage systems includes a fixed construction cost and a proportional cost (related to storage volume). For a storage system with cover, a fixed average additional covering cost is applied. The stored manure retains more than 99% of its original weight, and the loss is mainly due to the emission of gas and evaporation.

Sand recovery systems are needed before AD to adjust the solid content by diluting the manure and increase the stream weight. Due to the limited cost data of the sand recovery system, we assume that both investment and operational costs are proportional to the processing amount (facility size). 42 The sand-recovered manure can be used for AD, which converts

manure into digestate and biogas, and in this analysis, generated biogas is converted to electricity by a generator. The investment cost of the digester is obtained using the so- called 6/10 power-law regression to capture the economies of scale.4 The investment cost of the electricity generator is set as 2/3 of the corresponding digester (typical estimate used in the literature43,44 The operational cost of the AD system includes maintenance cost (which represents 10% of invest- ment) and biogas cleaning cost, which is proportional to the amount of biogas generated. The investment and operational cost of solid-liquid separation equipment (screw press) is obtained from the literature, and the cost of UF/RO technology is obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S.

Supply Chain Model. We developed an SC model that aims to systematically make decisions on which technologies to deploy and at which location. The model also makes a decision on transport of products (e.g., raw manure) and derived products (e.g., recovered nutrients) between geographical locations. In other words, the SC can be seen as a cooperative system that can exchange products across farms in order to minimize costs and mitigate environmental impacts.

The SC optimization model used is extended from that in ref 46 by integrating multiple economic and environmental metrics; this allows us to evaluate the performance of the SC from multiple perspectives.

In the SC model, we have a set of geographical nodes (locations), a set of materials, and a set of technologies (technology pathways). Materials can be transported between geographical nodes and transformed in technologies, and technologies can be installed in certain geographical nodes. The main constraints in the optimization model include material balances and conversion constraints at each location, operational capacity constraints, and logical constraints for technology selection and placement.

The SC model also calculates the total (system-wide) TEA and LCA metrics for the entire SC. These metrics contain impacts associated with technology pathways and trans- portation operations. The decision variables of the SC model include product transport flows and technology selection and placement. The decision variables are a combination of continuous (e.g., flows) and discrete (e.g., selection) variables; the SC model is thus a mixed-integer optimization problem. By adjusting the decision variables, the SC model seeks to minimize the TEA and LCA metrics simultaneously. In other words, the SC problem has multiple objectives that need to be minimized simultaneously. The SC model explored in this work yields large-scale optimization problems that are computationally expensive to solve; for more details, the readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

Conflict Analysis and Resolution. To facilitate the discussion, we denote the collection of decision variables of the SC model as X, and the system-wide TEA and LCA metrics as f1(x),f2(x), ,fk(x), where k = III + 1. We further denote the feasible set of the SC model as X, which is defined by constraints (S15)-(S29).

The economic and environmental metrics considered can be conflicting (decreasing one increases the other). Such conflicts (trade-offs) can be addressed using multiobjective optimiza- tion techniques. These techniques traditionally aim to compute the so-called Pareto frontier (PF). The PF contains all solutions that cannot be improved in the sense that one cannot further improve an objective without sacrificing another

objective. The PF enables visualization of trade-offs between different metrics (but this is difficult when more than three metrics are explored). A final decision is often selected from the PF based on expert knowledge or preferences by a decision-maker. This choice can be ambiguous because it relies on the priorities of the decision-maker (what metrics are prioritized). Moreover, to obtain the entire PF, it is necessary to solve an extremely large number of optimization problems (e.g., by putting different weights or budgets on the different metrics). For instance, in the multistage e-constraint method,4 47 one solves the following set of problems:

Here, Ej is some predefined threshold for objective fi(x). This method is not computationally feasible in our context because computing a single point on the PF requires solving the entire SC problem, and the number of points needed to describe the complete PF grows exponentially as the number of metrics increases. Sampling strategies are also often necessary to capture points on different regions of the PF, which can require additional computational time. Evolutionary algorithms have also been proposed to reduce the computational complexity, such as the NSGA-II and MOPSO method. 48,49 These algorithms do not guarantee optimality, and they are not suitable for solving large-scale problems. 50

An alternative (and more tractable) approach to selecting a solution from the PF is to use a conflict resolution framework. Here, we use a technique known as the utopia-tracking method, which aims to find a solution that balances all the metrics. 51,52 The main idea is to identify the best possible value of each metric to obtain what is known as the utopia point. In most cases, the utopia point is not achievable (nonreachable) since there are inherent conflicts between the different metrics. Instead, this approach aims to find a solution along the PF that is closest to the utopia point; this decision is known as the optimal compromise solution (OCS).

The first step in the utopia-tracking method is to determine the best possible performance of each metric by solving the k problems indicated by eq 2. The optimal objective values for those problems are denoted as fi, f, ...) fk, and the combination of them (i.e., [fi, f. fill is called the utopia point.

Metrics typically have different scales (e.g., different physical units), which makes it difficult to minimize the distance to the utopia point. As such, it is important to scale the metrics properly; this is done by finding the maximum values for the metrics on the PF (i.e., Nadir points). For problems with more than a couple of metrics, the Nadir point can be approximated by using a pay-off matrix:

The overall conflict resolution procedure is illustrated in Figure 1. This approach consists of the utopia point computation, pay-off matrix computation, and OCS computa- tion. The procedure is illustrated graphically in Figure 2 for a

setting with a couple of metrics. The overall procedure needs to solve the entire SC model a total of k2 + 1 times; each of these instances is a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) problem. In the case study considered in the following section, the entire procedure can take weeks to complete. This highlights the complexity involved in systematically assessing trade-offs in manure management.
28: The computational framework proposed includes the following components: quantification of environmental and economic metrics, supply chain model, and conflict resolution procedure. The environmental and economic metrics for diverse manure management pathways are evaluated using LCA and TEA approaches. These metrics are then used in an SC optimization model that makes decisions on technology selection/sizing/ placement, transportation, and production. Trade-offs are explored by solving the SC model repetitively in order to minimize/maximize individual metrics and construct a pay-off matrix. A conflict resolution procedure is then used to identify an optimal compromise solution that best balances the individual metrics. We note that the main methodological novelty of this study is to combine multiple computational components to quantify and resolve the conflicts between many economic and environmental metrics.

Life Cycle Assessment. An LCA model³ was used to estimate (i) GHG emissions, (ii) NH3 emissions, and (iii) depletion of fossil fuels (DFF) for dairy and beef manure management pathways. These environmental metrics include manure collection, storage, processing, and land application steps. In addition, nutrients (N, P, and K) reaching the land were tracked. We conducted analysis for a dairy farm with 2306 animal units (1 AU = 1000 pound of animal) that manages 1000 lactating cows, 605 growing heifers, and 286 mature heifers and dry cows) and for a beed farm with 2478 AU that manages 1,000 bulls and cows, 210 replacement cattle, 750 stocker cattle and 750 finishing cattle. Manure character- istics were estimated for each animal type for dairy33 and beef34 and aggregated for the herd (Table S1).

A total of 11 technology pathways were modeled for both dairy and beef farms (Table 1). Manure is collected from the barn in all pathways and then is either directly land applied, stored prior to land application, or processed -via sand
Table 1. Summary of the Operational Steps Included in Each Technology Pathway

technology|processing unit|storage type|land application method 1|none|none|surface 2|none|natural crust|surface 3|sand recovery|uncovered|surface 4|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|surface 5|sand recovery + AD + SLS|uncovered|surface 6|sand recovery + SLS|uncovered|surface 7|none|covered|injection 8|sand recovery + AD|covered|surface 9|sand recovery + AD|uncovered|injection 10|sand recovery + AD|covered|injection 11|sand recovery + AD + SLS + UF + RO|uncovered|surface

recovery, AD, mechanical SLS, and liquid manure processing to clean water (SLS + UF + RO)- and then land applied. All technical and economic data for the clean water system throughout all stages (SLS, UF and RO) was obtained from a real-life operation system at a dairy facility in the U.S. Manure storage is considered with and without a liner cover and manure is land applied by surface broadcast and injection. Table 1 presents a summary of each pathway; in this context, a technology is defined as a pathway containing different manure handling operation units.

GHG emissions (expressed in kg CO2-eq) include CH4, direct and indirect N2O from manure, and fossil carbon dioxide (CO2) from the combustion of fossil fuels used on- farm. Characterization factors for CH4 and N2O are 28 and 265, respectively, for a 100 year horizon. NH3 (expressed in 35 kg) is emitted from manure during collection, storage, and land application, and DFF (expressed in MJ) originates from the use of grid electricity and diesel for machinery operation.

Manure handling operations include collection, storage, and land application steps. Manure is collected daily with a skid steer that operates with diesel. Manure, digestate, and separated manure/digestate liquids and solids are in an open storage for six months before land application in April and October. A mineralization rate (conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium36) of 16% is assumed for the given storage duration. An organic crust is modeled in technology scenarios without manure processing due to higher total solids in manure, which decreases CH4 and NH3 emissions but 37 increases N2O emissions. Liner covers of the storage system (a lined pond) are also modeled. Manure is agitated and then land-applied by surface broadcast with diesel applicators or with manure injection to mitigate NH3 emissions. In other words, land application requires hauling and thus consumes fossil fuels and generates carbon emissions.

Mechanical SLS is modeled using a screw press that runs on electricity with separation efficiencies reported in Aguirre- Villegas et al. A plug-flow digester is used to produce biogas 38 considering a 28-day retention time. Electricity is generated from biogas (65% CH4 with a lower heating value of 36 MJ/ m³) at 35% and 50% electric and thermal efficiency, respectively. We note that the final CH4 production is assumed on the basis of typical volumetric compositions, which includes all losses as it is based on empirical data. 39 The digester is heated using 20% of the thermal energy generated during electricity production. It is assumed that the remaining thermal

energy is lost. In addition, the digester demands 17% of the daily produced electricity. Total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN) is increased by 35% due to mineralization during the digestion process. It is assumed that the produced electricity 37 is used on a farm with surplus injected into the electricity grid. This electricity is renewable and offsets both the life cycle GHG emissions and the DFF embedded in grid electricity. The offsets are expressed as metric benefits (negative GHG and DFF).

We consider a pathway that generates clean water from manure (we refer to this as the clean water pathway). This system requires prior AD and consists of three separation systems set up in series: a centrifuge, a UF system, and an RO system. Centrifugation achieves higher separation efficiencies than screw pressing. After centrifugation, the solid fraction is stored for six months before land application and the liquid digestate is further separated into an ultrafiltration concentrate (UFC) stream and an ultrafiltration permeate (UFP) stream (see Table S2 for separation efficiencies). Equations and emission factors used to estimate GHG, NH3 emissions and DFF, and total environmental metrics for the 11 technologies are presented in Supporting Information.

We highlight that different pathways can have steps in common; for instance, AD is a common step in many of them. This highlights the fact that there are complex dependencies between technologies and intermediary products. We also highlight that all pathways generate a nutrient-rich stream that needs to be disposed of. The nature of these streams is different (e.g., contain less or more water) and can thus be transported to a different geographical location to mitigate nutrient pollution (e.g., from nutrient-rich to nutrient-deficient regions)

29: In this section, we demonstrate the applicability of the proposed computational framework by targeting a case study in Wisconsin,
Study Region: Upper Yahara Watershed. The map in Figure 3 shows the geographical location and boundary of the

Figure 3. Geographical boundary and agricultural activities in the Upper Yahara Watershed: red circles are farms with the size representing the amount of manure produced annually, and green areas represent croplands with dark green representing high cropland density.

study region, as well as the location of croplands and farms (beef and dairy). The Upper Yahara Watershed (Lake Mendota basin) has been used as a case study to evaluate the impact of manure storage, 55 manure pelletization, and nutrient recovery technologies. 57,58

The study region is affected by nutrient pollution due to intensified agricultural activities; Dane County has made continuous efforts to reduce nutrient pollution (especially P loading) to improve water quality in the lakes (e.g., nutrient pollution leads to harmful algal blooms, or HABs). To highlight the degree of nutrient pollution in the region, we define the nutrient balance index (NBI) as the ratio of nutrient applied to land to the amount of nutrient that is removed by crops. This implies that when NBI is greater than one, the nutrient will accumulate in the region and potentially create nutrient pollution issues while, when NBI is less than one, it indicates that there is a nutrient deficiency in the soil (overall soil fertility may be declining and therefore is not sustainable in the long-run). The NBI values for P in the Upper Yahara Watershed in 2012 and 2013, were estimated to be 1.95 and 1.35, respectively. This means that this region has a significant P imbalance.

Previous studies have found that, to reduce P loading and HABs, the most effective strategies are manure storage (shift land application time), manure transportation (shift location for land application), and SLS (concentrate nutrients for improved transportation). Unfortunately, these strategies can have a negative impact on other environmental metrics. For example, fossil fuels are consumed in the transport and processing of manure; in addition, manure storage leads to significant CH4 emissions38 (due to the anaerobic environment created when liquid and slurry manure are stored). Moreover, the crops considered in the region have different nutrient demand levels. If we consider all the key nutrients for crop growth, such as N and potassium (K), we estimated the NBI for N, P, and K to be 1.27, 1.65, and 1.47, respectively (in 2017). As a result, all nutrients are imbalanced; although P is the main nutrient that causes nutrient pollution in inland waterbodies, it has been reported that N contributes to nutrient pollution in groundwater. In addition, up to 70% of the excreted N in manure can be emitted as NH3 in livestock operations. These losses represent 80-90% of the global anthropogenic NH3 emissions that can redeposit and lead to impaired waterways, or further transform to particulate matter or N2O. As a result, animal agriculture farmers, researchers, and policy makers need to be aware of current emission levels to target future mitigation strategies. A suitable technology deployment solution should thus take all these metrics and conflicts into account and enable a more comprehensive decision-making process. The data and setting of the supply chain system in this study region are given in Supporting Information.

Scenario Description. For the baseline scenario (Scenario 1), we only include prevalent technology pathways (technol- ogy 1-10 in Table 1) and we study the UF/RO pathway separately. All value-added products (such as electricity) are sold directly under current market prices (without any subsidies from the government). The conflict resolution procedure described in Figure 1 is followed to compute the OCS. Because we have a total of 7 metrics, we need to solve 7 MILP problems to find the utopia point, 42 MILP problems to construct the pay-off matrix, and one additional MILP to obtain the OCS. This gives a total of 50 MILP problems.

We note that, with the specified study area and corresponding data, each SC model is a large-scale MILP problem. For example, a typical model contains 548 727 continuous variables, 9280 binary variables, and 313 699 linear constraints. The SC models were implemented using the Julia- based modeling framework JuMP (Version 0.21.2) and were solved with Gurobi (Version 9.0.3). The models are computationally challenging; solving the entire set of 50 MILPs require weeks to complete. This illustrates the complexity involved in resolving conflicts in complex SCs such as those arising in manure management. Some of the problems were not solved to optimality within the imposed 35 h time limit. The time limit is set to balance the trade-off between the solution quality and data storage issues coming from large branching of MILPs. In such cases, the best solution found (usually with an optimality gap less than 10%) is used for analysis. We note that these suboptimal solutions are close to the potential optimal ones and will not impact our main analysis and conclusions.
In Scenario 2, we introduce a UF/RO technology pathway to produce clean water from manure (technology 11 in Table 1) and determine the impact of this technology on the LCA
Table 2. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 1

Figure 4. Optimal compromised solugion (OCS) designs for Scenario 1, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

and TEA metrics. Scenario 3 considers prevalent technologies with subsidies introduced into the SC in the form of energy credits (i.e., the system will obtain additional profits when it recovers renewable energy from livestock manure). For Scenarios 2 and 3, the same conflict resolution procedure is followed to analyze how external factors can manipulate the OCS and provide insights to policy-makers and stakeholders. Scenario 3 is included in the Supporting Information due to space limit.

To highlight the degree of nutrient pollution in the region, we define the nutrient balance index (NBI) as the ratio of nutrient applied to land to the amount of nutrient that is removed by crops. This implies that when NBI is greater than one, the nutrient will accumulate in the region and potentially create nutrient pollution issues while, when NBI is less than one, it indicates that there is a nutrient deficiency in the soil (overall soil fertility may be declining and therefore is not sustainable in the long-run). The NBI values for P in the Upper Yahara Watershed in 2012 and 2013, were estimated to be 1.95 and 1.35, respectively. This means that this region has a significant P imbalance.

Previous studies have found that, to reduce P loading and HABs, the most effective strategies are manure storage (shift land application time), manure transportation (shift location for land application), and SLS (concentrate nutrients for improved transportation). Unfortunately, these strategies can have a negative impact on other environmental metrics. For example, fossil fuels are consumed in the transport and processing of manure; in addition, manure storage leads to significant CH4 emissions38 (due to the anaerobic environment created when liquid and slurry manure are stored). Moreover, the crops considered in the region have different nutrient demand levels. If we consider all the key nutrients for crop growth, such as N and potassium (K), we estimated the NBI for N, P, and K to be 1.27, 1.65, and 1.47, respectively (in 2017). As a result, all nutrients are imbalanced; although P is the main nutrient that causes nutrient pollution in inland waterbodies, it has been reported that N contributes to nutrient pollution in groundwater. In addition, up to 70% of the excreted N in manure can be emitted as NH3 in livestock operations. These losses represent 80-90% of the global anthropogenic NH3 emissions that can redeposit and lead to impaired waterways, or further transform to particulate matter or N2O. As a result, animal agriculture farmers, researchers, and policy makers need to be aware of current emission levels to target future mitigation strategies. A suitable technology deployment solution should thus take all these metrics and conflicts into account and enable a more comprehensive decision-making process. The data and setting of the supply chain system in this study region are given in Supporting Information.

Scenario Description. For the baseline scenario (Scenario 1), we only include prevalent technology pathways (technol- ogy 1-10 in Table 1) and we study the UF/RO pathway separately. All value-added products (such as electricity) are sold directly under current market prices (without any subsidies from the government). The conflict resolution procedure described in Figure 1 is followed to compute the OCS. Because we have a total of 7 metrics, we need to solve 7 MILP problems to find the utopia point, 42 MILP problems to construct the pay-off matrix, and one additional MILP to obtain the OCS. This gives a total of 50 MILP problems.

We note that, with the specified study area and corresponding data, each SC model is a large-scale MILP problem. For example, a typical model contains 548 727 continuous variables, 9280 binary variables, and 313 699 linear constraints. The SC models were implemented using the Julia- based modeling framework JuMP (Version 0.21.2) and were solved with Gurobi (Version 9.0.3). The models are computationally challenging; solving the entire set of 50 MILPs require weeks to complete. This illustrates the complexity involved in resolving conflicts in complex SCs such as those arising in manure management. Some of the problems were not solved to optimality within the imposed 35 h time limit. The time limit is set to balance the trade-off between the solution quality and data storage issues coming from large branching of MILPs. In such cases, the best solution found (usually with an optimality gap less than 10%) is used for analysis. We note that these suboptimal solutions are close to the potential optimal ones and will not impact our main analysis and conclusions.
In Scenario 2, we introduce a UF/RO technology pathway to produce clean water from manure (technology 11 in Table 1) and determine the impact of this technology on the LCA
Table 2. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 1

Figure 4. Optimal compromised solugion (OCS) designs for Scenario 1, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

and TEA metrics. Scenario 3 considers prevalent technologies with subsidies introduced into the SC in the form of energy credits (i.e., the system will obtain additional profits when it recovers renewable energy from livestock manure). For Scenarios 2 and 3, the same conflict resolution procedure is followed to analyze how external factors can manipulate the OCS and provide insights to policy-makers and stakeholders. Scenario 3 is included in the Supporting Information due to space limit.

This implies that when NBI is greater than one, the nutrient will accumulate in the region and potentially create nutrient pollution issues while, when NBI is less than one, it indicates that there is a nutrient deficiency in the soil (overall soil fertility may be declining and therefore is not sustainable in the long-run). The NBI values for P in the Upper Yahara Watershed in 2012 and 2013, were estimated to be 1.95 and 1.35, respectively. This means that this region has a significant P imbalance.

Previous studies have found that, to reduce P loading and HABs, the most effective strategies are manure storage (shift land application time), manure transportation (shift location for land application), and SLS (concentrate nutrients for improved transportation). Unfortunately, these strategies can have a negative impact on other environmental metrics. For example, fossil fuels are consumed in the transport and processing of manure; in addition, manure storage leads to significant CH4 emissions38 (due to the anaerobic environment created when liquid and slurry manure are stored). Moreover, the crops considered in the region have different nutrient demand levels. If we consider all the key nutrients for crop growth, such as N and potassium (K), we estimated the NBI for N, P, and K to be 1.27, 1.65, and 1.47, respectively (in 2017). As a result, all nutrients are imbalanced; although P is the main nutrient that causes nutrient pollution in inland waterbodies, it has been reported that N contributes to nutrient pollution in groundwater. In addition, up to 70% of the excreted N in manure can be emitted as NH3 in livestock operations. These losses represent 80-90% of the global anthropogenic NH3 emissions that can redeposit and lead to impaired waterways, or further transform to particulate matter or N2O. As a result, animal agriculture farmers, researchers, and policy makers need to be aware of current emission levels to target future mitigation strategies. A suitable technology deployment solution should thus take all these metrics and conflicts into account and enable a more comprehensive decision-making process. The data and setting of the supply chain system in this study region are given in Supporting Information.

Scenario Description. For the baseline scenario (Scenario 1), we only include prevalent technology pathways (technol- ogy 1-10 in Table 1) and we study the UF/RO pathway separately. All value-added products (such as electricity) are sold directly under current market prices (without any subsidies from the government). The conflict resolution procedure described in Figure 1 is followed to compute the OCS. Because we have a total of 7 metrics, we need to solve 7 MILP problems to find the utopia point, 42 MILP problems to construct the pay-off matrix, and one additional MILP to obtain the OCS. This gives a total of 50 MILP problems.

We note that, with the specified study area and corresponding data, each SC model is a large-scale MILP problem. For example, a typical model contains 548 727 continuous variables, 9280 binary variables, and 313 699 linear constraints. The SC models were implemented using the Julia- based modeling framework JuMP (Version 0.21.2) and were solved with Gurobi (Version 9.0.3). The models are computationally challenging; solving the entire set of 50 MILPs require weeks to complete. This illustrates the complexity involved in resolving conflicts in complex SCs such as those arising in manure management. Some of the problems were not solved to optimality within the imposed 35 h time limit. The time limit is set to balance the trade-off between the solution quality and data storage issues coming from large branching of MILPs. In such cases, the best solution found (usually with an optimality gap less than 10%) is used for analysis. We note that these suboptimal solutions are close to the potential optimal ones and will not impact our main analysis and conclusions.
In Scenario 2, we introduce a UF/RO technology pathway to produce clean water from manure (technology 11 in Table 1) and determine the impact of this technology on the LCA
Table 2. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 1

Figure 4. Optimal compromised solugion (OCS) designs for Scenario 1, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.

and TEA metrics. Scenario 3 considers prevalent technologies with subsidies introduced into the SC in the form of energy credits (i.e., the system will obtain additional profits when it recovers renewable energy from livestock manure). For Scenarios 2 and 3, the same conflict resolution procedure is followed to analyze how external factors can manipulate the OCS and provide insights to policy-makers and stakeholders. Scenario 3 is included in the Supporting Information due to space limit.
37: Base Scenario: Payoff Matrix and Optimal Compro- mise Solution. Table 2 presents the pay-off matrix for Scenario 1. Here, the utopia point consists of the diagonal elements (highlighted as *), and the nadir point consists of the maximum values of each column (highlighted as .). We use the notation M1-M2 to denote the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

The pay-off matrix and utopia point provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limit behavior of each metric (best and worst values) and the global conflicts between metrics. For instance, we can see that the total cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. In other words, it is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts with traditional technologies. This makes sense, as mitigating environmental impacts requires investment. Within the environmental metrics, the GHG shows a strong conflict with nutrient reductions (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows weak conflicts with nutrient and costs and a strong conflict with NH3. The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are
Table 3. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 2

listed, OCS values represent the performance of the corresponding metric in the original units, and the scaled values are obtained from (6), which have a unit of one and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

The overall cost obtained in the OCS is 22 million USD/ year, which is close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. This means that, by increasing the cost, the system has economic budget flexibility available to improve all other environmental metrics and can thus resolve the conflicts among those metrics. We can see that the environmental metrics are close to those of the utopia point. The GHG emissions value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although this is higher than the utopia value (236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than values obtained with other methods (e.g., for problems Cost-GHG, Cost-NH3, and Cost-N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. To reduce various environmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies, including AD, SLS, and covered storage. In other words, the system diversifies investment in order to hit all the metrics involved. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.
40: Base Scenario: Payoff Matrix and Optimal Compro- mise Solution. Table 2 presents the pay-off matrix for Scenario 1. Here, the utopia point consists of the diagonal elements (highlighted as *), and the nadir point consists of the maximum values of each column (highlighted as .). We use the notation M1-M2 to denote the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

The pay-off matrix and utopia point provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limit behavior of each metric (best and worst values) and the global conflicts between metrics. For instance, we can see that the total cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. In other words, it is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts with traditional technologies. This makes sense, as mitigating environmental impacts requires investment. Within the environmental metrics, the GHG shows a strong conflict with nutrient reductions (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows weak conflicts with nutrient and costs and a strong conflict with NH3. The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are
Table 3. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 2

listed, OCS values represent the performance of the corresponding metric in the original units, and the scaled values are obtained from (6), which have a unit of one and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

The overall cost obtained in the OCS is 22 million USD/ year, which is close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. This means that, by increasing the cost, the system has economic budget flexibility available to improve all other environmental metrics and can thus resolve the conflicts among those metrics. We can see that the environmental metrics are close to those of the utopia point. The GHG emissions value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although this is higher than the utopia value (236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than values obtained with other methods (e.g., for problems Cost-GHG, Cost-NH3, and Cost-N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. To reduce various environmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies, including AD, SLS, and covered storage. In other words, the system diversifies investment in order to hit all the metrics involved. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.
43: Base Scenario: Payoff Matrix and Optimal Compro- mise Solution. Table 2 presents the pay-off matrix for Scenario 1. Here, the utopia point consists of the diagonal elements (highlighted as *), and the nadir point consists of the maximum values of each column (highlighted as .). We use the notation M1-M2 to denote the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

The pay-off matrix and utopia point provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limit behavior of each metric (best and worst values) and the global conflicts between metrics. For instance, we can see that the total cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. In other words, it is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts with traditional technologies. This makes sense, as mitigating environmental impacts requires investment. Within the environmental metrics, the GHG shows a strong conflict with nutrient reductions (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows weak conflicts with nutrient and costs and a strong conflict with NH3. The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are
Table 3. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 2

listed, OCS values represent the performance of the corresponding metric in the original units, and the scaled values are obtained from (6), which have a unit of one and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

The overall cost obtained in the OCS is 22 million USD/ year, which is close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. This means that, by increasing the cost, the system has economic budget flexibility available to improve all other environmental metrics and can thus resolve the conflicts among those metrics. We can see that the environmental metrics are close to those of the utopia point. The GHG emissions value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although this is higher than the utopia value (236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than values obtained with other methods (e.g., for problems Cost-GHG, Cost-NH3, and Cost-N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region.
