The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. To reduce various environmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies, including AD, SLS, and covered storage. In other words, the system diversifies investment in order to hit all the metrics involved. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.
46: Base Scenario: Payoff Matrix and Optimal Compro- mise Solution. Table 2 presents the pay-off matrix for Scenario 1. Here, the utopia point consists of the diagonal elements (highlighted as *), and the nadir point consists of the maximum values of each column (highlighted as .). We use the notation M1-M2 to denote the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

The pay-off matrix and utopia point provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limit behavior of each metric (best and worst values) and the global conflicts between metrics. For instance, we can see that the total cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. In other words, it is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts with traditional technologies. This makes sense, as mitigating environmental impacts requires investment. Within the environmental metrics, the GHG shows a strong conflict with nutrient reductions (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows weak conflicts with nutrient and costs and a strong conflict with NH3. The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are

The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are
Table 3. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 2

listed, OCS values represent the performance of the corresponding metric in the original units, and the scaled values are obtained from (6), which have a unit of one and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

The overall cost obtained in the OCS is 22 million USD/ year, which is close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. This means that, by increasing the cost, the system has economic budget flexibility available to improve all other environmental metrics and can thus resolve the conflicts among those metrics. We can see that the environmental metrics are close to those of the utopia point. The GHG emissions value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although this is higher than the utopia value (236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than values obtained with other methods (e.g., for problems Cost-GHG, Cost-NH3, and Cost-N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. To reduce various environmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies, including AD, SLS, and covered storage. In other words, the system diversifies investment in order to hit all the metrics involved. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.

For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

51: Base Scenario: Payoff Matrix and Optimal Compro- mise Solution. Table 2 presents the pay-off matrix for Scenario 1. Here, the utopia point consists of the diagonal elements (highlighted as *), and the nadir point consists of the maximum values of each column (highlighted as .). We use the notation M1-M2 to denote the problem with metric M1 as the primary objective and metric M2 as the secondary objective. For example, Cost-GHG represents the problem that keeps the cost at a minimum value and minimizes GHG emissions (row 1 and column 2). The notation M1-M1 represent the problem with M as the only objective. In addition, we use the notation M1-* to represent the set of problems whose primary objective is M1.

The pay-off matrix and utopia point provide useful insights to decision-makers, especially regarding the limit behavior of each metric (best and worst values) and the global conflicts between metrics. For instance, we can see that the total cost conflicts with all other environmental objectives. In other words, it is not profitable to reduce any of the analyzed environmental impacts with traditional technologies. This makes sense, as mitigating environmental impacts requires investment. Within the environmental metrics, the GHG shows a strong conflict with nutrient reductions (especially N and K), but a weak conflict with DFF. Similarly, DFF shows weak conflicts with nutrient and costs and a strong conflict with NH3. The nutrient metrics (N/P/K) show a weak conflict with one another; this indicates that these metrics can be potentially improved simultaneously. For detailed analysis of subproblems presenting in the payoff matrix, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The system can make extreme or irrational decisions when only one or two metrics are considered (and other metrics are ignored). For example, fully focusing on minimization of GHG emissions might lead to high nutrient pollution. The determination of an OCS seeks to avoid these issues by simultaneously capturing all metrics. The metrics obtained for the OCS for Scenario 1 are presented in Table 2. Here, minimum and maximum values from the payoff matrix are
Table 3. Pay-Off Matrix, Utopia Point, and OCS of Scenario 2

listed, OCS values represent the performance of the corresponding metric in the original units, and the scaled values are obtained from (6), which have a unit of one and represent the relative distance along the certain direction.

The overall cost obtained in the OCS is 22 million USD/ year, which is close to the maximum value in the pay-off matrix. This means that, by increasing the cost, the system has economic budget flexibility available to improve all other environmental metrics and can thus resolve the conflicts among those metrics. We can see that the environmental metrics are close to those of the utopia point. The GHG emissions value is 3776 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year. Although this is higher than the utopia value (236 metric tonnes of CO2-eq per year), it is also an order of magnitude smaller than values obtained with other methods (e.g., for problems Cost-GHG, Cost-NH3, and Cost-N). The DFF value is -670 GJ per year, which is only 5% greater than that of the utopia value. A negative value indicates that the SC system produces renewable power that can replace fossil-based grid electricity. The net nutrient release values are 677, 29, and 1,449 metric tonnes per year for N, P, and K, respectively. All of these values are close to the utopia values, but we observe that none of the nutrients is perfectly balanced. We thus see that OCS is seeking to strike a balance between all the objectives.

In the scaled objective space, the distance between OCS and the utopia point is 2.01 (with optimality gap of 12% after 35 h of solving). Half of the distance is contributed by the cost (1.01), and the six environmental objectives contribute the remaining distances. This again shows that, for the OCS, the cost needs to be sufficiently large so that the environmental impact can be reduced and then achieve overall smallest distance to the utopia point. Specifically for GHG, DFF, P, and K, all distances contributed by them are less than 0.1.

The SC design under the OCS is shown in Figure 4. To reduce various environmental impacts, the system tends to install different types of processing technologies, including AD, SLS, and covered storage. In other words, the system diversifies investment in order to hit all the metrics involved. There are 31 farms with AD and SLS technologies installed (technology 5, blue dots) and 19 farms with AD and covered storage installed (technology 9, gray dots). There is one farm with ADs (brown dot) and one farm with covered storage (purple dot). We also found that most technologies are installed at medium or large farms, with an average farm size of 590 AU. There are also 23 farms directly sending the raw manure to nearby lands, most of which are small farms and the

unprocessed manure only occupies 2.5% of all manure in the study region. The average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, and liquid products are 1.93 km, 2.12 km, 15.95 km, and 1.60 km, respectively. Addition- ally, there is a mix of surface application and injection application used in croplands. Around 41.4% of manure in the system is applied by injection. Due to the mineralization of organic N after the digestion process, using a cover in storage is not sufficient to effectively prevent NH3 emissions and needs to be coupled with injection as a method for land application. Manure injection can reduce the NH3 emissions of land application by 95%, and reduce the overall NH3 emissions by 45%. However, injection increases GHG emissions by 10% consistently with results presented by Chadwick et al.6 as it creates the necessary conditions to convert nitrate to N2O. Therefore, the mixed land application method is a technology pathway that seeks to resolve the inherent conflict between GHG and NH3 emissions.

To summarize, in Scenario 1, most manure is processed by technologies, where 96.9% is processed by AD, 52% is processed by SLS, and 45.4% is stored with coverage. Among those technologies, AD can create benefits in GHG and DFF reduction, SLS can further provide flexibility in product transportation and reduce nutrient losses, and covered storage systems are preferred for decreasing NH3 and DFF. The average technology sizes are 600, 530, and 680 AU, respectively. These technologies contributes 65.5% to the system cost and 89.4% to the overall GHG emissions, while transportation contributes 34.5% and 10.6% to these metrics, respectively.

Impact of New Technologies. In Scenario 2, the UF/RO technology pathway (technology 11) is added into the system. This technology can provide high efficiency in nutrient separation to obtain clean water (but requires higher investment and operational costs). The centrifuge separator produces solids with high nutrient content (around 30% more concentrated than screw press). The UF and RO byproduct streams are assumed to be used as a liquid digestate products in the SC and can be directly applied on cropland. The UFC and ROC streams have a lower total solids content than the liquid digestate in this system, as a centrifuge, with higher separation efficiencies, is considered for the clean water separation system VS a screw press for the AD system (Table S2). As a result, it is less economic to transport UFC and ROC due to their larger water content. Clean water is approximately one-third of the initial digestate volume and can be directly discharged or consumed by animals (reducing transportation

Farms Farms with AD & Coverage Transportation of Livestock Waste Transportation of Slurry Digestate Aggregated Croplands Farms with Clean Water Tech Transportation of Liquid Digestate Transportation of UFC/ROC Farms with AD & SLS Farms with AD Transportation of Solid Digestate
Figure 5. OCS designs for Scenario 2, where the left figure shows transportation flows of raw manure and the right figure shows transportation flows of derived products.
costs). The pay-off matrix obtained under Scenario 2 is presented in Table 3.

The treatment of manure to clean water mainly influences they way nutrients are processed and does not interfere with other metrics (the technologies in Scenario 1 are still selected). As a result, values for cost, GHG, NH3, and DFF metrics are similar to those of Scenario 1. This also indicates that a specific technology reducing an environmental metric in Scenario 1 will also achieve such an effect in Scenario 2. With this in mind, even if we consider nutrient as a secondary objective, only transportation can be adjusted and the new UF/RO technology will not be selected (resulting in similar values in the pay-off matrix). After the clean water technology is installed, N and K are still not balanced, but minimum nutrient release values are achieved due to higher nutrient concen- tration in the demanded solids; this increases the overall system cost by 28.9% and 43.5%, respectively. Introducing the new technology only brings additional degrees of freedom to balance P, as this was already balanced in Scenario 1. Therefore, some of the problems corresponding to P minimization in Scenario 2 show better performance than Scenario 1. Both problem N-P and K-P are perfectly P balanced, and problems P-* also show lower NH3 emissions and DFF consumption. This indicates that the new technology provides flexibility to balance nutrients. For detailed analysis, readers can refer to the Supporting Information.

The OCS of Scenario 2 was estimated by using the conflict resolution procedure; the results are shown in Table 3. The cost of the OCS is increased by around 3 million USD/year compared with Scenario 1, mainly due to the installment of new technologies. The net N and K releases are reduced by 16.1% and 17.3%, respectively, and P can be perfectly balanced. On the other hand, the GHG and DFF values are increased slightly, mainly due to the decreased biogas production. Generally, the new technology makes nutrient recovery easier and endows the ability to achieve lower nutrient release. If we look at the scaled objective space, the new OCS shows a similar composition to Scenario 1, where the cost contributes almost half of the total distance, and all environmental metrics are properly improved with increased investment. While some of the metrics have slightly worse

performance, the new OCS moves toward ideal nutrient management, and it is also closer to the utopia point. The scaled distance to the new utopia point is 1.66 (with optimality gap of 14.9% after 35 h of solving). If we use the minimum and maximum values in Scenario 1 for scaling, the distance is 1.73, which is decreased by 14.3%.

The SC design obtained with the OCS is shown in Figure 5. It is clear that the system installs different types of technologies to balance conflicts between environmental metrics. In total, 53 farms have AD installed and can process 99.2% of manure, 7 of which are accompanied by uncovered storage systems (technology 8), 33 farms have covered storage systems (technology 9), and 13 of which are equipped with SLS (technology 5). The average sizes of the technologies are 350, 510, and 440 AU, respectively. There are four large clean water technologies (average size of 2050 AU) installed that can process 25.9% of manure in the region. Compared with Scenario 1, where 52% of manure is processed by AD and SLS, the new clean water technology replaced almost half of the nutrient recovery task. The total amount of unprocessed manure is also decreased from 2.5% to 0.8% under this new setting. The number of AD systems is increased in order to balance out the additional GHG emissions in transporting raw manure to centralized facilities. For this scenario, the average transportation distance of raw manure, digestate, solid products, liquid products, and UFC/ROC are 2.50 km, 1.62 km, 11.83 km, 0.98 km, and 2.50 km, respectively. The transportation, investment, and operational cost is increased by 12.6%, 14.4%, and 6.2%, respectively. The additional technology costs are mainly introduced by the UF/RO technologies used. The transportation cost is increased because more manure in the system is processed and also because the more concentrated solid is sold to external customers and the system needs to move diluted streams to balance nutrients in the region.

53: We presented a computational framework that uses techno- economic analysis and life-cycle assessment to evaluate diverse economic and environmental impacts of manure processing technologies. These impacts are captured in a multiobjective

supply chain optimization problem that makes decisions on technology selection/placement and product transportation. The framework also incorporates a conflict analysis and resolution procedure to systematically navigate trade-offs and resolve conflicts. We applied this framework in a case study of Upper Yahara Watershed in the State of Wisconsin. The pay- off matrix generated indicates that the system will not process any manure if the system aims to minimize cost (because land application is the least expensive option). We also found that there are strong conflicts between cost and environmental metrics (GHG emissions, NH3 emissions, and nutrient pollution). We also found that phosphorus can be balanced more easily in the region than nitrogen and potassium. Some complex conflicts between environmental metrics are also revealed in the pay-off matrix (such as GHG emissions and nutrient pollution, and GHG emissions and NH3 emissions). The optimal compromise solution obtained shows that, to achieve the closes solution to the utopia point, the system cost needs to be increased significantly (the system needs economic budget flexibility) SO that other environmental metrics can be improved together. The decisions obtained also involve a mixed use of different technologies (anaerobic digestion and solid-liquid separation) and strategies regarding storage, transportation, and land application to improve environmental metrics efficiently. We also tested the impact of novel technologies and incentives on the optimal comprise solution. We found that the treatment of manure using wastewater purification technologies is able to replace most solid-liquid separation technologies in the optimal compromise solution (despite of its relatively high costs) due to its better separation efficiency. We also found that renewable energy incentives are not able to improve the environmental metrics of the system, but they can mitigate the conflict between GHG emissions and cost. The optimal compromise solution also shows that the system can generate a profit, with slight changes of environmental metrics.

For future work, we plan to further improve the metric selection process. In our current analysis of livestock manure management, the type of environmental metrics included are mainly derived from expert knowledge. While these metrics generally represent the most important aspects in the system, from the perspective of optimization, some of those metrics can be correlated because of their complex interactions. Therefore, some mechanism for prior selection of metrics could be beneficial. We will also seek to understand the potential barriers of deploying optimal compromise decisions in those systems. Our results show that some incentives might only improve economic performance (and not environmental outcomes); as such, we will use our methodology to investigate incentive strategies that can displace the entire set of metrics (e.g., nutrient credits and carbon taxes). We are also interested in identifying optimal compromise solutions for predefined economic budgets (as those are typically of interest to stakeholders); obtaining such types of solutions will require a significant amount of computation. We are also interested in investigating algorithms to handle the high computational complexity of the problems under study (which currently take weeks to solve). Finally, we are interested in developing a software tool that can support policy-makers in decision- making of farm operations at a systems level. We envision this could help identify proper technologies and waste processing strategies to best resolve the conflicts between economic and environmental metrics in a specified study region.
